The human eye, a sensitive organ, functions like a camera with a lens system that forms an image on the retina. Light enters through the cornea, with the crystalline lens adjusting focal length via ciliary muscles for clear vision at varying distances (accommodation). The near point is typically 25 cm, and the far point is infinity for a normal eye.

Common vision defects include myopia (nearsightedness), hypermetropia (farsightedness), and presbyopia, where the eye's refractive power is altered. Myopia occurs when the focal length is too short, causing distant objects to be blurred. Hypermetropia happens when the focal length is too long, blurring nearby objects. Presbyopia is age-related loss of accommodation. These defects are corrected using appropriate spherical lenses that adjust the light's focus onto the retina. Cataract, a clouding of the lens, also impairs vision and can be surgically treated.
